item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's working in itself and isn't trying to kill people - as of yet - so I like it.

On the other hand the "words between quotes" distract from the subject and detract from the scientific archive theme. These logs and documentations are meant to describe the entities, their stories and containment procedures with precision and effectiveness to any foundation's employee reading them, wherever and whenever that happens.
For that reason, using the expresion "scratch 'n' sniff" to describe its anomalous effect doesn't make it. Expressing that a smell is emitted when someone rubs the picture is sufficient to convey the desired meaning precisely and effectively and doesn't require to be aware of what a "scratch 'n' sniff" can be, since I assume it is a real thing.

For the same reason, this:

many low-end LCDs and most panels older than █ years have failed to yield a detectable smell.

Two things.
Whatever that redacted number is, it means that LCD screens became able to convey the anomalous effect at a certain year, which isn't how the industry works. Since all sorts of screens of all sorts of resolution are made for all sorts of usages, you wouldn't remotely observe a transition that sudden that you could sum it up with a single year. A minimal resolution would work better here, in my opinion, but that's a minor issue.
However, that same number isn't something that we hsould fine in articles and I'm surprised that nobody had issues with it. Whatever that number is means that it has to be changed every year, which is ludicrous. A date would make it, but datations relative to the reader's point of view only work with text styles which temporal context is obvious because they are short-lived or precisely dated like logs, news, conversations…
What would you think if you were reading in the wikipedia article of an animal that it had been discovered "17 years ago"?

Although the concept is interesting, because of these two things and because I would have appreciated to see the idea digged a bit more, I can't vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://www.flickr.com/photos/jcapaldi/3435779732/

Image Source, CC BY-2.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A skip which is NOT nightmare fuel. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: well, with mobile devices and touch screens becoming more and more prevalent, it's not that big a surprise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1 DON'T G3T 1T, 1 KN3W HOW TO DO THIS 3V3R S1NC3 1 B3C4M3 BL1ND.

In all seriousness, I think its interesting and I totally did not scratch nor did I proceed to sniff after said scratching of my computer screen.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is a CC-BY-2.0 image from Flickr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, I didn't have high hopes for this one, what with it having a singular, rather tame anomalous effect. However, the final addendum introduced the possibility of different compounds coming into contact with the image resulting in different scents-that's a very interesting concept to me. With that, and the high quality of the writing, I'm upvoting this. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has plenty of merit for containment either way. This particular JPEG may smell like flowers, but the final thought of the article, about the sweaty fingers, shows that it might be transmuting molecules into something else. What if the next JPEG by this one's creator, changed it to Anthrax, or some other sort of biological terror? This could simply be a precursory test to a much sinister motive. Containing how many people have the anomolous version, what ruins the effect, and tracing it's source could lead to avoiding impending disaster. I know you already mentioned something along this line in your post, but it more of seems to be worried about hidden effects of this exact Jpeg. I'd be more worried about, what if more appear, with worse effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tried the JPEG.
It didn't work.
:,(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost a physical synesthesia-like effect. Touch becomes a smell. Definitely strange and weird. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this quite a bit. It's a strange, interesting and quirky item married to a well-written article. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Before reading the caption, I scratched the picture. ._.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Screw that. +1 for truth, +2 for Foghorn Leghorn. NEVER enough Foggy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something anomalous that is not creepy in the creepy sense. However, most people would be creeped out if they thought about it. just how am I smelling this? How does it know I touched the screen. Who's watching me to release the smell?

Upvoted for a skillful, creepy non-creepy entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to everyone who's commented; I've read and digested your words, then excreted a miasma of edits which may or may not have improved the article. I feel some more edits are due tomorrow; I'll keep a bucket handy.

Edit: There we go, I've edited all the bits I was meaning to edit; that should address most of the problems pointed out so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me chuckle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This comment: +1 for truth, -1 for Foghorn Leghorn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for well-executed original idea.
This further illustrates1 that not all SCP subjects need to be horror stories. SCP-914, SCP-908 and SCP-1021 are examples of concepts that are more sci-fi than horror.

Footnotes
1. See, I say, see what I did there, son? That's a pun. A play on words. That's a joke, son. A flag waver. You're built too low. The fast ones go over your head. Ya got a hole in your glove. I keep pitchin' 'em and you keep missin' 'em. Ya gotta keep your eye on the ball. Eye. Ball. I almost had a gag, son. Joke, that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry? It only makes scents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I first saw this, I though "anomalous item log" (and I see you did too), but reading the entire thing through and thinking about it, it makes a bit of sense. It's not particularly dangerous (that we know of), it doesn't cause any kind of mass panic, and if it somehow got out of containment, it might make people think "huh, did I get some air freshener on my screen?" at worst. Still worth containing, but it's nice to see some thought put into something that WON'T cause an XK event for once. Nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-Weird.
-Makes no sense according to modern physics.
-Not Keter.
-Smells good.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the first addendum:

using "nearest nighbour"

Am assuming that's a typo.

This is pretty well written, and I like the bits of technical data, but it just boils down to a digital scratch 'n' sniff. Yeah, it's unusual, but this is more something I'd expect to find in the log of anomalous artifacts rather than a full-fledged SCP article. There's nothing intriguing about it. My only thought after reading this is, "So what?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should, not has to. We just had an issue with this not to long ago when someone went through and changed a bunch of pictures without asking. It has never been policy to force anyone to host on our site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Offsite linking is bad internet etiquette, against the policies of many websites, and the Guide specifically mentions that images should be uploaded to individual article pages. If you intend to change policy, then make it official.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm… not sure at all why I like this so much.

Good writing, weird concept carried well, tight execution that neatly carries the full idea without need for supplementary pages, and just enough jargon to be believable but still readable. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know many people who regularly rub their computer screens.

yes you do

you just don't realize it

*heavy breathing*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He probably had a speck of dust or something stuck on his monitor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to see some scientific testing of the effect. Does scratching the image actually produce detectable chemical odorants, or is it purely a perceptual effect?

Otherwise I think it's cool just the way it is :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scritch…scritch…hey! It works! Cool!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the article itself is good, I just have one question:

SCP-1029 was discovered by Agent █████ on a web image gallery at URL [DATA EXPUNGED] on ██/██/████. Agent █████ noticed the image's reaction to being rubbed and saved a copy for further study.

I don't know about you, but I don't know many people who regularly rub their computer screens. Was it being passed around a website or forum with instructions on it? Because if so that would probably make a bit more sense. Additionally, wouldn't the Foundation look into whoever hosted the site, or more importantly who put the image up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image file jargon makes this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quirky and interestingly weird. It needn't be dangerous. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Parroting RhettSarlin and Sorts, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a neat idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I think it should be left as-is as far as functionality/creepy. Not every SCP has to be creepy; it DOES have to have a reason to be kept from public knowledge. The fact that it seems to have an outright breaking of the rules of reality (Maybe it's some paranormal effect; maybe it's a memetic agent with unbelievably precise parameters) is enough in-and-of itself to keep it in Foundation storage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Simple and clever, with no unnecessary danger added. That said, the tone is a bit off in places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, edited, hopefully it reads a tad better now. (I probably should have put it aside for review tomorrow rather than posting within 3 hours of the original idea! Oh well, next time…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Raven, please do not yell at people for something they aren't doing wrong. It's entirely an authors choice where to host their picture. Please do not fix that without author consent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My two cents? Making it creepy makes no sense. Best if anomaly is only the scents.

I'm sorry. Couldn't resist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must admit I originally thought of putting this in the anomalous items log instead, but wanted to say more about it than the format of the log allows. I can't claim to be much good at "creepy", to be honest; or indeed creative writing at all. If anyone can suggest how I might creepy-fy this item, I'm all ears. (Hey, how about an object that's all ears…?) Yes, you could say that the mere fact that it works should overturn pretty much all of science and make us question the very fabric of the universe… but then all the SCPs do that as a matter of course. ;-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's… Pretty much how I feel, actually. I'll hold off voting until it gets some clean-up, but for now it looks like it has potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
upvoting for being interesting and a new idea….

some will probably argue that as there's no element of danger or horror to this that it isnt something that merits containment. i argue that it would, however, simply because it is unexplained and merits study into how it produces its effects, plus as it is paranormal its potential for unanticipated negative effects is unknown, and thus containment is merited to mitigate them.

whether or not this is a good SCP article is another matter altogether.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea seems interesting, if a bit bland, but the tone seems a bit off.

One thing that people have noted before is overuse of quoted terms. You use "scratch'n'sniff" at least three times, and it gets silly at that point. Try leading off with

[…] when rubbed or scratched, […] effect similar to that of a "scratch and sniff" surface.

and then subsequently referring to the effect merely as "its anomalous effect".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, didn't notice an instruction to that effect. Will do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the love of God, read the How to Write a SCP instructions, where it says to PLEASE UPLOAD YOUR IMAGE TO YOUR PAGE INSTEAD OF OFFSITE LINKING. This is the third article I've fixed this week.
